attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The user is reporting two issues they encountered when running Cocos Creator:

1. **Problem 1**: Missing sound effects (main_bgm, fg_bgm) - they've already added these, but need to decide whether to fix this in the template project or elsewhere.

2. **Problem 2**: The game board has 5 columns (COL=5), but SlotReels' NORMAL_COLUMNS, m_reelYOffsetProperty, m_reelPositionOffset, and other column-related properties are still calculated using 6 columns.

This is a discovery/problem identification. The user has found a mismatch between the configured column count and the hardcoded values in the SlotReels implementation.

Let me create an observation for this:

**Type**: discovery (they're learning about existing system issues)
**Title**: Column count mismatch between config and SlotReels implementation
**Subtitle**: Game board configured for 5 columns but SlotReels hardcoded properties use 6 columns
**Facts**:
- Game board COL value is set to 5
- SlotReels NORMAL_COLUMNS property uses 6 columns
- SlotReels m_reelYOffsetProperty uses 6 column calculation
- SlotReels m_reelPositionOffset uses 6 column calculation
- Missing audio files main_bgm and fg_bgm were added as workaround
**Narrative**: When testing in Cocos Creator, two issues were discovered. First, audio files main_bgm and fg_bgm were missing and have been temporarily added. Second, there's a column count mismatch where the game board is configured for 5 columns (COL=5), but SlotReels implementation has hardcoded values for 6 columns in NORMAL_COLUMNS, m_reelYOffsetProperty, and m_reelPositionOffset. This creates inconsistency between the configuration and the actual reel rendering logic.
**Concepts**: This is fundamentally a problem-solution scenario—the configuration and implementation are out of sync, which is a gotcha that likely occurred when the column count was changed without updating all dependent properties in SlotReels. **Files**: The SlotReels component and its configuration are the key areas needing alignment, though specific file paths weren't mentioned.

content.thinking:
This grep search shows where all these hardcoded arrays are used throughout the codebase. Let me analyze:

1. **SCATTER_COLUMNS** in Game_Define.ts (line 203): defined as [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
   - Used in NearWinDetector.ts at lines 71 and 136
   - Referenced in a comment at line 8 of NearWinDetector.ts

2. **NORMAL_COLUMNS** in SlotReels.ts (line 18): defined as [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
   - Used at line 922 in SlotReels.ts: `this.SpinAll( dt, NORMAL_COLUMNS );`

3. **m_reelYOffsetProperty** in SlotReels.ts (line 53): [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
   - Used at line 574: `this.m_reelYOffset[ col ] = ( col < this.m_reelYOffsetProperty.length ) ? this.m_reelYOffsetProperty[ col ] : 0;`

4. **m_reelPositionOffset** in SlotReels.ts (line 267): Vec3 array
   - Used at line 1757: `+ this.m_reelPositionOffset[ col ].x`

So we now have a complete picture of where these hardcoded 6-column arrays are defined and how they're used. The NearWinDetector also depends on SCATTER_COLUMNS, so fixing that will affect near-win detection logic.

/**\\n     * @ch 旋轉\\n     * @param dt 時間\\n     */\\n    Spin( dt )\\n    {\\n        this.m_time = this.ToolsStrip( this.m_time + dt );\\n        if ( this.IsStop )\\n        {\\n            return;\\n        }\\n\\n        this.SpinAll( dt, NORMAL_COLUMNS );\\n\\n        if ( this.StopTime === 0 || this.m_time <= this.StopTime )\\n        {\\n            return;\\n        }\\n\\n    this.m_reelSoundId = null;\\n        }\\n    }\\n\\n    /**\\n     * @ch 旋轉上、中、下所有輪帶，第二個參數為控制單輪\\n     * @param dt 時間\\n     * @param cols 列索引 (0 ~ Game_Define.COL-1)\\n     */\\n    SpinAll( dt, ];\\n\\n            // 計算時間\\n            let curTime = this.m_time;\\n            let riseTime = this.m_totalRiseTime;\\n            let moveLength = ( ( ( curTime - riseTime ) % spinTime ) / 所有 `curTime < NaN` 為 false，與下方 short-circuit `reelStopTime === null ||` 等效，不影響行為\\n        const riseTimeCol = this.m_riseTime * col;\\n        const riseTimeColPlus1 = riseTimeCol + + this.m_spinBufferTime02;\\n\\n        if ( curTime < riseTimeCol ) // 抬起續力表演\\n        {\\n            return;\\n        }\\n        else if ( curTime < riseTimeColPlus1 )\\n        {\\n            let moveLength = ( ( curTime - riseTimeCol ) / this.m_riseTime ) * this.m_upLength;\\n            this.m_tempVec3.set( x, y + moveLength, 0 );\\n            target.SetPosition( this.m_tempVec3 );\\n content.thinking:
The user has edited SlotReels.ts to fix the NORMAL_COLUMNS constant. Instead of being hardcoded to [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5], it now dynamically generates the array based on Game_Define.COL using Array.from().

This is a bugfix that makes the code more maintainable and fixes the column count mismatch issue.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed NORMAL_COLUMNS to derive from Game_Define.COL dynamically</title>
  <subtitle>Changed hardcoded 6-element array to dynamic generation matching configured column count</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>SlotReels.ts line 18 NORMAL_COLUMNS constant changed from hardcoded [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] to Array.from({length: Game_Define.COL}, (_, i) => i)</fact>
    <fact>Dynamic generation ensures NORMAL_COLUMNS stays synchronized with Game_Define.COL configuration</fact>
    <fact>Added Chinese comment explaining the change prevents future desync when board size changes</fact>
    <fact>Fixes column count mismatch where COL=5 but NORMAL_COLUMNS had 6 elements</fact>
    <fact>NORMAL_COLUMNS passed to SpinAll method at line 922 now operates on correct number of columns</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed the first of four hardcoded 6-column arrays by making NORMAL_COLUMNS dynamically derive from Game_Define.COL configuration. The constant was changed from a hardcoded [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] array to Array.from({length: Game_Define.COL}, (_, i) => i), which generates [0, 1, 2, 3, 4] when COL=5. This ensures the SpinAll method (line 922) correctly iterates over only the configured columns, preventing attempts to spin non-existent column 5. Added a Chinese comment explaining the rationale: avoiding desync when board dimensions change in the future. This pattern should be applied to the remaining three hardcoded arrays: m_reelYOffsetProperty, m_reelPositionOffset in SlotReels.ts, and SCATTER_COLUMNS in Game_Define.ts.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>
  <files_modified>

   this.m_reelSoundId = null;\\n        }\\n    }\\n\\n    /**\\n     * @ch 旋轉上、中、下所有輪帶，第二個參數為控制單輪\\n     * @param dt 時間\\n     * @param cols 列索引 (0 ~ Game_Define.COL-1)\\n     */\\n    SpinAll( dt, 所有 `curTime < NaN` 為 false，與下方 short-circuit `reelStopTime === null ||` 等效，不影響行為\\n        const riseTimeCol = this.m_riseTime * col;\\n        const riseTimeColPlus1 = riseTimeCol + content.thinking:
The user is investigating the missing audio files (main_bgm, fg_bgm) that they encountered when running Cocos Creator. The grep shows:

1. In uk_739 (another project):
   - AudioManager.ts defines MG_Bgm: { FileName: "main_bgm", Clip: null }
   - AudioManager.ts defines FG_Bgm: { FileName: "fg_bgm", Clip: null }
   - Actual files: fg_bgm.m4a and main_bgm.m4a (lowercase)

2. In uk_917 (current project):
   - The user has added files:
     - FG_BGM.m4a (uppercase)
     - fg_bgm.mp3
     - main_bgm.mp3
     - MG_BGM.m4a (uppercase)
     - MG_BGM_Extra.m4a

The issue is likely a filename case mismatch: AudioManager expects "main_bgm" and "fg_bgm" (lowercase) but the user added uppercase versions (FG_BGM.m4a, MG_BGM.m4a). Also, there's an mp3 vs m4a format mismatch.

This is a discovery about the audio file naming inconsistency.

  <subtitle>AudioManager expects lowercase main_bgm/fg_bgm but project has uppercase MG_BGM.m4a/FG_BGM.m4a files</subtitle>

    <fact>uk_917 also has lowercase mp3 versions fg_bgm.mp3 and main_bgm.mp3 in addition to uppercase m4a versions</fact>
    <fact>Filename case mismatch causes AudioManager to fail loading BGM audio clips on case-sensitive systems</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Comparison with the uk_739_wrath_of_thunder_client reference project reveals an audio file naming inconsistency. The AudioManager.ts configuration expects lowercase filenames "main_bgm" and "fg_bgm", as evidenced by the FileName properties (lines 36-37). The reference project uk_739 correctly has lowercase files fg_bgm.m4a and main_bgm.m4a. However, the current uk_917 project has uppercase files FG_BGM.m4a and MG_BGM.m4a, plus lowercase mp3 versions. This case mismatch would cause the AudioManager to fail loading audio clips on case-sensitive file systems or when Cocos Creator performs strict filename matching. The issue explains the missing BGM audio problem encountered during testing. Solution requires either renaming the uppercase m4a files to lowercase or updating AudioManager configuration to use uppercase filenames.</narrative>
